trajectory_summary: The call was effectively over before Ella had a chance to run her process. Ronald entered with a binary free/not-free frame and hardened it three times in 90 seconds. Ella's responses — a feature-list pitch, a process deflection, and a verification pivot — were all procedurally reactive and never engaged the emotional root of the objection. The concrete-roof DQ at 02:06 ended the call, but the trajectory was already pointing to a dead end from the first exchange.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is listed as 'unknown.' Based on the transcript, the correct tag is 'disqualified — concrete roof' with a secondary note that the call was already structurally failing before the DQ was reached. No appointment was booked or attempted.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: So you're not gonna be paying, like, any money that you're already spending with your utility company. So what we have going on is we're doing a bill swap program. That's where we switch out your current electric bill for a cheaper solar payment plan. So a lot of our customers really like this program because we have no down payments, no upfront cost, and then we also have no installation fees as well.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Ronald handed Ella a live inversion opportunity three times in 90 seconds and she responded with a feature list, a process deflection, and a verification pivot — all variations of the same non-answer. Regardless of lead type, the inversion move (flip the 'free' frame onto the current utility bill, then ask what he's paying) is the only tool with a chance of breaking a binary-frame customer. This is the single highest-leverage coaching gap on this call: Ella does not yet have the inversion in her toolkit, and without it, every hostile binary-frame customer will produce the same outcome.

key_moments.quote: So you're not gonna be paying, like, any money that you're already spending with your utility company. So what we have going on is we're doing a bill swap program. That's where we switch out your current electric bill for a cheaper solar payment plan. So a lot of our customers really like this program because we have no down payments, no upfront cost, and then we also have no installation fees as well.
key_moments.stewart_read: Ella's first substantive move is a feature-list pitch delivered before she knows Ronald's bill amount. The bill swap framing is directionally correct — it gestures at the right concept — but it is executed as a feature dump rather than an emotional anchor. She never asks what Ronald is currently paying, which means the 'cheaper solar payment' claim has no number to land against. The schema's capture-then-flip sequence (ask + acknowledge + flip) is skipped entirely: Ella goes straight to pitch without the ask. Strategic read: could Ella be intentionally deferring bill capture to get past the free/not-free objection first? Possible, but the feature list that follows is not a setup for a later flip — it is a standalone pitch that creates no curiosity and anchors nothing. Reactive read holds.

key_moments.quote: So how much is this whole thing gonna cost?
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the second touch of the price-before-process demand, arriving mid-pitch. Ronald is not asking a clarifying question — he is interrupting to restate his original frame. Ella's response ('That's what we're trying to figure out for you. So first, we just have to get you out the estimate and information') is a process deflection that is procedurally accurate but emotionally inert. It does not engage the demand at a new angle. The schema calls for 3+ angles per objection; Ella's second response is a variation of her first (we need more info before we can give you numbers). No inversion, no curiosity creation, no bill pain anchor.

key_moments.quote: No. I wanna get the I wanna get the numbers before we start.
key_moments.stewart_read: Third touch of the same demand. Ella's response is a verification pivot — she moves to address confirmation rather than engaging the objection at a new angle. This is the point where the schema's multi_angle_resilience standard is most clearly unmet: three touches, three procedural deflections, zero emotional engagement. The customer's escalating repetition ('No. I wanna get the I wanna get the numbers before we start') signals that he is not confused about the process — he is refusing it. A third procedural explanation will not move him.

key_moments.quote: Hey, Ronald. This is Ella with ION. I just got a request here that you're looking at solar for your home in Nevada. Is that right?
key_moments.stewart_read: Ella opens by stating the home is in Nevada. At 01:31, the address confirmed is in Virginia ('961 Laguna Street in Parham, Virginia'). This is a state mismatch in the intro — Ella's lead data either had the wrong state or she misread it. Ronald did not correct her at 00:04, which means the error went unaddressed for over 90 seconds. On a call where the customer is already hostile and testing legitimacy, a state mismatch in the opening line is a credibility risk. Ronald may not have noticed, but if he had, it would have confirmed his scam suspicion immediately.
